speaker: interviewer
text: Helen, who is in this photo, if we start from left to right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that photo we got after the war from America, and that is my grandfather, my father's father.

speaker: interviewer
text: He's on the right-hand side.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, with the beard. And then is my father in lighter suit. That's my father. And that's his brothers and one sister behind. One sister. But no one is alive anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know. It was long before the war, I believe. Because my-- I don't think-- maybe when my father married, it was, the photo taken. My grandfather is old. Or it might be later, because I remember my grandfather, when I was very little, [INAUDIBLE], yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where was it? In Cracow.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Cracow.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Cracow, yes. The whole family there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is my father, my father in his uniform.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe that photo was taken before I was born, because it's the Army-- he was in a war. The first World War, that was finishing in '18, '19. It was about in 19-something, that started off the war. I believe then he was engaged, maybe, to my mother. It was many years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: From America. We have-- his two brother were still-- when we arrive here to Australia, the two brother were still alive in America. And that's how I really got it, contact. My sister did everything. She just all did it, all the correspondence with all family, that was my sister. And she got even, you know, the cousin, what is-- from America. Even to-- many, not many years ago, she was still contact. I lost everything, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my mother and my father. It was sent from America to us, after the war. And I don't know even if I was born when the pictures was taken. I don't think so, it was maybe before-- after the war, but I-- first war, but I don't know when it was taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where are they?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was engaged for a few years before she married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where are they in this photo, do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Cracow. It was some studio most probably. I believe so, yes. That's my father, was looking like a [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about this photo.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a group of us, in Tonie. That was first year of the war. And was happiest time for me. I didn't know that war is-- what is the war. And that was my brother. It was my boyfriend. So I can call it boyfriend.

speaker: interviewer
text: Starting from--

speaker: interviewee
text: The dark one, at top.

speaker: interviewer
text: Starting from say, left to right, tell me who the people are.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. First it was [INAUDIBLE] I don't know his name. He was friend of-- and then next one was Dorca, Dorca [INAUDIBLE]. Her parents had the-- factory in Cracow. That was my brother, and that was [? Tadek ?] leaning on me. That was [INAUDIBLE] And next to me was Romek [INAUDIBLE]. And then-- no, first was my sister with him. And I was the last one, is with Romek. The last one is me. I can see now. I was fit, much fitter than I am now. I was bigger, big bust. And that was very happy time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my son, my husband, and I think Louise there. And that was in [INAUDIBLE], first time when we went to Australia, maybe a year, first year in Australia, or second year. Yes, because my daughter was-- and I was two and a half when we came here. That was most probably the first year in Australia, in [INAUDIBLE]. It was a big garden there, very big house, very big yard. We had a lot of trees. And we work-- I was working in the garden. I love it. I have time for everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was my brother in the middle. First is my sister, and that's me. My sister was much taller. I don't know how it doesn't show on the photo, because she was taller than I. And that was taken in Tonie again, first year of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your sister is on the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, first was my sister, and then my brother, then myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe that Dorca [INAUDIBLE], the girl [INAUDIBLE] with us, that she sent us, she-- she is dead now, most probably. She used to live in Vienna, and she sent it to us or to my sister. You see, all contact with the people was my sister. She was corresponding with all family, with everybody. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: From left to right, tell me who is in this photo.

speaker: interviewee
text: First was my husband, my younger daughter Frances, myself, my son-in-law Guy Blay, his wife, that's my daughter Anna. And that was we celebrate my wedding anniversary. I don't know which one. Anna was pregnant on that photo. That's-- she was pregnant since very soon after she married. I don't know when it was. What anniversary, I don't remember. She had baby already. You know, when she--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is in Israel, in Jerusalem. King David's Hotel.

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eh, two and a half, three years ago when the film was made, I think. And it's-- first is my husband. That is Schindler's girlfriend, Marta. And that is Emilie Schindler and myself. I don't know if she recognized me, but she was very nice to me. She shake my hand and she was very sweet woman. And that was in King David's Hotel. We have lectures for three days with Spielberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, if we go from left to right, tell me who's in the photo.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was my husband, I think first, I'm not sure. Myself, Guy is sitting there, and Paul is sitting on the floor and Kate, that's Paul's girlfriend. And that is I don't know then. I can't see properly. You see, I think it's Danny. I maybe need my glasses, because it's very little photo. I know it's everybody. It's-- Danny with his wife Cindy. And it's Paul with his girlfriend, and it's my brother-- oh, first it's my brother-in-law, Josef Gross. That's-- myself, my husband, Anna, Frances, Clive, everybody. All my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not long ago, maybe six months ago, I think. Even the dog is there in it. I think so. That was not long ago. I think it was a time when somebody was leaving. Ben, I think, was leaving. To-- that's when he made this photo. Not long-- at Anna's place.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Helen, from left to right could you introduce your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first, my three granddaughter, middle one is Carly, Gabby, and Jessica And that this is my son-in-law, my daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you just tell us their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Clive Sondheim, Fran Sondheim, and myself. That's my husband, my dear husband for 53 years. And that this is my older daughter Anna, and my middle grandson Paul The other two grandson are not here. Danny and Benji is away.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not having grandparents of my own, I'm really very, very grateful to be here, and have my children have their two grandparents here in good health. And it's a wonderful thing that they survived and I'm very grateful.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I think also it's important for both of us to see our parents being able to tell their stories, when, for so many years, when we were brought up as children, they couldn't talk about it. So this is a wonderful opportunity for them to tell us and their grandchildren and future generations of what they've been through, very important.

speaker: interviewee
text: I suppose for me it's been great to finally understand that my grandparents had a life before here. And I feel lucky to know about it and to understand how beautiful their life was before, and the sad things that happened, and the family that aren't here now.
